title: MLOps: The Operations Checklist
concept: MLOps is the engineering discipline of managing a machine learning model's full lifecycle, from training and testing through deployment, monitoring, and retraining.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What's a downside of continuous retraining?

options.text: It's expensive and risks quickly corrupting the model if bad data streams in.
It can never detect drift at all.
It only works for regression models.

exercises.explain: Correct! Continuous retraining is costly and vulnerable to bad incoming data.

cards.title: Flying a plane vs. building it
cards.body: Building an airplane engine (training a model) is a huge achievement. But keeping that plane flying safely for years — fueling it, inspecting it, fixing worn parts — is an entirely different, ongoing job. Machine learning has the exact same split: building a model is one job, and running it forever is another.

cards.title: MLOps: Managing the full lifecycle
cards.body: MLOps (Machine Learning Operations) is the engineering discipline covering a model's entire lifecycle: training, testing, deploying, serving, monitoring, and retraining — treating ML models like living systems, not one-time projects.

cards.title: Gardening, not sculpture
cards.body: Training a model once and never touching it again is like carving a marble statue — a finished, static object. MLOps treats a model like a garden instead: it needs continuous watering (monitoring), weeding (drift correction), and occasional replanting (retraining) to stay healthy.

cards.title: The MLOps checklist
cards.body: A mature MLOps pipeline includes: 1. **Training** (build the model), 2. **Testing** (validate performance), 3. **Serialization** (save it to a file), 4. **Serving** (expose it via an API), 5. **Monitoring** (watch for drift), 6. **Retraining** (refresh it when needed).

cards.title: Training the model is not the finish line
cards.body: Beginners often think the hardest part of machine learning is training an accurate model. In real production systems, the majority of ongoing engineering effort actually goes into MLOps — the deployment, monitoring, and maintenance work that keeps a model useful for years.

cards.title: The MLOps Lifecycle
cards.body: The six stages of a mature ML pipeline:

left.text:
1. Train the model.
2. Test and validate it.
3. Serialize (save) it.

right.text:
4. Serve it via API.
5. Monitor for drift.
6. Retrain when needed.

options.text: The engineering discipline of managing a model's full lifecycle: training, deployment, monitoring, and retraining.
A specific type of decision tree algorithm.
A method for cleaning missing values in a dataset.
A file format used to store model weights.

exercises.explain: Correct! MLOps covers the entire operational lifecycle of a model, not just training.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In real production systems, training an accurate model is usually the hardest and most time-consuming part of the entire process.
exercises.explain: False! Ongoing deployment, monitoring, and maintenance (MLOps) is usually a much bigger, continuous effort than the initial training.

exercises.sentence: MLOps treats a machine learning model less like a finished sculpture and more like a ___ that needs ongoing care.
exercises.explain: The gardening analogy captures how models need continuous care, unlike a one-time finished sculpture.

exercises.prompt: Which activity is part of MLOps, beyond just training the initial model?

options.text: Setting up automated monitoring dashboards that alert the team when a deployed model's prediction patterns shift.
Choosing which features to use during the very first training run.
Splitting data into train and test sets before training begins.

exercises.explain: Correct! Ongoing monitoring after deployment is a core MLOps activity, distinct from the initial training steps.

exercises.prompt: A startup trains an excellent fraud detection model with 98% accuracy in the lab, but has no plan for saving it, serving it via an API, monitoring it, or retraining it. What is missing from their process, and why does it matter?

options.text: They are missing MLOps: without serialization, serving, monitoring, and retraining plans, the model can't be reliably used or maintained once deployed, no matter how accurate it was in the lab.
Nothing is missing — a high lab accuracy score guarantees success in production forever.
They need to retrain the model to be even more accurate before doing anything else.
They should switch to an entirely different algorithm.

exercises.explain: Exactly! A great lab model is worthless without the full MLOps pipeline around it — serialization, serving, monitoring, and retraining are what keep a model reliable and valuable over time in the real world. Congratulations on completing the Model Drift unit! Next, let's dive into the technical details of actually saving a model!
